subsystem: Ceph RADOS Gateway (RGW) S3-compatible object-storage frontend. The single function under review is RGWGetObj_ObjStore_S3::send_response_data, the handler that builds and emits the HTTP response for an S3 GET-object request: it computes the status line, dumps every response header (error, range, content-length, ETag, user metadata, object-lock, tags, and client-requested response-* overrides), then streams the object body. It is a response-serialization boundary that reflects both stored object attributes and client-supplied query parameters back into the HTTP response.
provenance: Identified as Ceph RGW from the class name RGWGetObj_ObjStore_S3 and method send_response_data(bufferlist&, off_t, off_t); from Ceph-specific types and helpers (bufferlist, ldout/ldpp_dout/dendl, JSONFormatter, encode_json, utime_t/real_clock, RGWObjTags/RGWObjectRetention/RGWObjectLegalHold); and from RGW macros/globals (RGW_SYS_PARAM_PREFIX, RGW_ATTR_PG_VER, RGW_ATTR_SOURCE_ZONE, RGW_ATTR_ETAG, RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX, RGW_ATTR_PREFIX, RGW_ATTR_CONTENT_TYPE, RGW_ATTR_TAGS, rgw_to_http_attrs). The banner names the file corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp and the in-code comment tags it primevul_validation_0383_vuln; the cwe79 hint (Cross-Site Scripting) is treated as untrusted and the code is judged on its merits.
trust_boundary: This is an internet-facing HTTP response builder. Two attacker-controllable sources feed it. (1) The S3 request query string, reached via s->info.args.get(p->param, &exists) (line 105) for the standard S3 response-* override parameters (response-content-type, response-content-disposition, etc.); the remote client fully controls both the presence and the string VALUE of each. (2) Stored object attributes in the attrs map (iterated at line 121), which were written by whoever uploaded the object or set its user metadata (x-amz-meta-*, ETag, content-type, tags, retention) — untrusted for cross-tenant/public buckets and for any value an uploader chose. Both sources are copied into response_attrs and emitted verbatim as HTTP header names/values (dump_header at 149/183) and, for content-type, into the Content-Type header (end_header at 192). The object body bl (line 202) is also attacker-influenced content. The only access-control gate present is the anonymous-caller rejection at lines 109-111.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The method emits one chunk of an S3 GET response. Parameters bl (the object data buffer), bl_ofs (offset within it), and bl_len (number of bytes to send) describe the body slice. Locals are declared up front because the function uses goto: content_type is a raw C pointer that will point into content_type_str (a std::string whose lifetime is the whole function, so a pointer into it stays valid until return); response_attrs accumulates header name->value pairs to emit; riter iterates them later; metadata_bl holds optional JSON-encoded metadata for system requests. The return type is int (an S3/errno-style status).

sent_header is a once-guard: on the first call the full header block is built; on subsequent calls (streaming further body chunks) control jumps straight to send_data so headers are emitted exactly once. The status code is chosen from custom_http_ret if the op set one, else from op_ret / STATUS_PARTIAL_CONTENT for range requests; set_req_state_err/dump_errno write the HTTP status. If op_ret is non-zero (an error), control skips the body/metadata population and jumps to done to emit just the accumulated headers. This goto structure is why all locals are declared before the first jump.

If the client sent a Range header (range_str set), the handler emits a Content-Range header using the parsed start/end and the object size. start/end derive from client input but are numeric and validated elsewhere; this step is a formatting call.

This block runs only for internal multisite/system requests (s->system_request) that ask for prepended metadata. It emits the object size, optionally zeroes total_len when only metadata is wanted (rgwx_stat), then JSON-encodes the full attrs map plus the modification time into metadata_bl, advertises its length in the Rgwx-Embedded-Metadata-Len header, and adds it to total_len so Content-Length covers the metadata prefix. This path requires system_request, so it is not reachable by an ordinary anonymous or authenticated public client.

Also gated on system_request. It emits internal multisite headers: the mtime epoch, a decoded placement-group version (pg_ver, uint64) and a source-zone short id (uint32). decode_attr_bl_single_value pulls typed scalars out of the stored attribute bufferlist with a default of 0 and logs (does not abort) on failure. Because these values are typed scalars printed as numbers, they are not free-form header-injection vectors; the block is only reachable by internal system requests.

Emits server-side-encryption response headers (crypt_http_responses, produced by the encryption layer), the Content-Length (total_len), Last-Modified, the version id (only if non-empty), and an object-type marker based on whether the object carries an append-part-number attribute. version_id and the object-type strings originate server-side; content-length and obj_size are numeric.

On success (op_ret == 0) the handler emits the ETag: for Swift large objects it uses the composited lo_etag, otherwise it reads the stored RGW_ATTR_ETAG attribute and dumps it. The ETag value comes from stored object state (attrs), i.e. from whoever wrote the object.

This is the S3 response-override feature. resp_attr_params is a static table mapping query-parameter names (p->param, e.g. response-content-disposition) to their target HTTP header (p->http_attr). For each one the client-supplied query VALUE is read into val via s->info.args.get. If present, the code first rejects anonymous callers (lines 109-111) — the only access control here. For every override except response-content-type, val is stored verbatim into response_attrs keyed by the target header name; for response-content-type it is stashed in content_type_str and content_type is pointed at its buffer. These strings are 100% attacker-controlled and will later be written into HTTP headers.

Now the handler walks every stored attribute. For attributes whose internal name maps to a standard HTTP header (rgw_to_http_attrs), and which were not already overridden by a response-* param, it copies the stored value into response_attrs under the mapped header name. The while loop strips trailing NUL bytes from the value (some attrs are stored NUL-padded): it shortens len and resizes the temporary string s until the last byte is non-zero. Note the local variable s shadows the request-state s used elsewhere, but within this block only the string is referenced. The resulting value flows into a response header.

Continuing the attribute switch: if this attr is the stored content type and no override already set content_type, content_type_str is filled from the stored value (rgw_bl_str converts the bufferlist to a string) and content_type points at it. The SLO indicator emits a fixed marker header. The user-custom-metadata branch matches attrs whose name begins with RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX, advances the name pointer past the shorter RGW_ATTR_PREFIX so the emitted header name keeps the x-amz-meta-... suffix, and dumps it as a header with the stored value iter->second. Both the header name (derived from the stored attribute key) and the value are uploader-controlled.

The remaining branches decode structured stored attributes: object tags (emitting only a numeric count header), object-lock retention (mode string + retain-until date), and legal hold (status). Each decode is wrapped in try/catch on buffer::error so a malformed stored blob logs and is skipped rather than throwing out of the handler — a deliberate robustness measure against corrupt/hostile stored attributes. The values emitted (retention mode, legal-hold status) come from server-defined enums via get_mode()/get_status(), not free-form strings, so they are lower-risk than the raw metadata copies above.

The done label is the common tail. It walks response_attrs and emits each accumulated header name/value via dump_header — this is where the client-supplied response-* override values (lines 113/128-134) and mapped stored attributes actually hit the wire. Then end_header finalizes the header block: for 304 Not Modified no body/content-type; otherwise the content type is defaulted to binary/octet-stream if still unset and passed to end_header, which writes the Content-Type header. If system metadata was JSON-encoded, it is written as the body. sent_header is set so later chunks skip all of this.

The send_data label streams the object body slice. If the request wants data (get_data) and there was no error, it writes bl_len bytes starting at bl.c_str() + bl_ofs. The pointer arithmetic assumes bl_ofs and bl_len fall within the bufferlist; these come from the read pipeline. On write failure it propagates the negative status, else returns 0. This is the only place the actual object content is emitted, after the header block chosen above (including the attacker-influenceable Content-Type).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The Content-Type of the response is taken directly from the client-controlled response-content-type query parameter (val, read at line 105) and later handed to end_header at line 192. A remote attacker can force the response to be served with, e.g., text/html or an XSS-executing MIME type. Combined with attacker-influenced object bodies (public buckets, user uploads, SVG/HTML content), a victim who follows a crafted GET URL has the object rendered and script executed in the origin of the RGW/S3 endpoint. This is the classic reflected/stored Cross-Site-Scripting via MIME override (CWE-79). The only gate is the anonymous rejection at 109-111, which does NOT stop an authenticated user from crafting a link that a victim's authenticated browser session then loads. The implementation must set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and ideally a restrictive Content-Disposition/CSP, so the browser cannot be coerced into executing object content.

response_attrs values are copied verbatim from client query parameters at line 113 (response-content-disposition, response-cache-control, response-expires, etc.) and from stored attributes at line 134, then emitted as raw header values here. If dump_header does not strip or reject CR/LF, an attacker who embeds \r\n in a response-* value (or in a stored attribute value) performs HTTP response-header injection / response splitting (CWE-113): injecting additional headers (e.g. Set-Cookie, a second Content-Type) or a fabricated body, which is itself an XSS and cache-poisoning primitive. This is a latent finding: the sink dump_header is not shown, so whether the value is sanitized must be confirmed in the implementation. The invariant is that no header value written here may contain control characters (CR, LF, NUL).

User custom metadata is reflected into response headers with BOTH an attacker-derived header NAME (the name pointer, taken from the stored attribute key with the prefix stripped) and an attacker-supplied VALUE (iter->second) at line 149. Metadata is written by whoever uploaded the object; for shared/public/cross-tenant buckets this is a different principal from the reader. If the stored key or value contains CR/LF or an HTML-dangerous payload, this becomes a stored header-injection / stored-XSS vector delivered to every reader (CWE-79 / CWE-113). Independently, the pointer bump name += sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX) - 1 assumes RGW_ATTR_PREFIX is a proper prefix of RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX so the shorter advance stays inside the string matched by the strncmp on the longer prefix; if that macro relationship ever changed, name could point past intended data. The implementation must sanitize both the emitted header name and value and confirm the prefix-length invariant.

When no response-content-type override was supplied, the Content-Type is taken from the STORED content-type attribute (rgw_bl_str(iter->second)) and flows to end_header at line 192. An uploader who set an object's content type to text/html (or any script-executing type) causes readers' browsers to render the object as HTML — the stored-XSS counterpart of the reflected override risk above (CWE-79). The mitigation is again nosniff / disposition controls at the header sink; the stored value alone should never be trusted to choose rendering behavior for a third-party reader.

The trailing-NUL trim only removes NUL bytes at the very end of a stored value; interior NULs, CR, or LF survive and are stored into response_attrs, then emitted as a header at line 183. So this loop is not a sanitizer for header injection — it addresses NUL padding, not control characters. If a reviewer assumes this normalizes the value they may miss that CR/LF in a mapped stored attribute still reaches the header sink. The loop itself is bounds-safe (len && guards the s[len-1] access and len only decreases), so there is no out-of-bounds read here; the risk is the false sense of sanitization plus the unfiltered value reaching dump_header.

what_could_go_wrong.code:
if (get_data && !op_ret) {
    int r = dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The body write uses bl.c_str() + bl_ofs for bl_len bytes with no visible check that bl_ofs >= 0, that bl_len >= 0, or that bl_ofs + bl_len <= bl.length(). off_t is signed, so a negative or oversized offset/length coming from the read/range pipeline would produce an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) or send unintended memory. This is a latent contract: the caller/range logic must guarantee the slice lies within the bufferlist. It is lower severity than the header-reflection issues because bl_ofs/bl_len are normally computed server-side from validated range state rather than taken raw from the client, but the bound should be confirmed.

summary: This RGW S3 GET-response builder reflects fully client-controlled query parameters (response-content-type and other response-* overrides) and uploader-controlled stored object attributes/metadata into HTTP response headers and the Content-Type, with the only access gate being an anonymous-caller rejection. The dominant risk is Cross-Site Scripting / HTTP response-header injection (CWE-79/CWE-113): an attacker can force the object to be served as text/html or inject header content, executing script in the endpoint origin. Safety depends entirely on the (unshown) dump_header/end_header sinks stripping control characters and on nosniff/disposition hardening.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: send_response_data(bufferlist& bl, off_t bl_ofs, off_t bl_len) signature (2-3)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; bl/bl_ofs/bl_len risk is captured under the dump_body slice finding, not the declaration itself.

content_type points into function-scoped content_type_str which is not modified after being set and outlives every use through line 192, so no dangling-pointer/use-after-free despite the .c_str() pattern.
Standard idempotent header-emission guard; single-threaded per response, no re-entrancy contract violated in the shown code.
Numeric status codes into set_req_state_err/dump_errno; not attacker free-form data.
Range values are numeric and validated by the range parser upstream; emitted as a formatted numeric header.
Gated on s->system_request (internal multisite), not reachable by public clients; total_len is uint64 and metadata length is server-bounded, no realistic overflow.
system_request-only; typed scalar values printed as numbers, not header-injection vectors, and decode failure is logged not fatal.
Values are server-generated (encryption layer, numeric length, fixed strings); version_id emitted only if non-empty.
ETag is a bounded hash-format value via dump_etag; lower-risk stored value, covered generically by the header-sanitization concern.
Client query values become header content; core untrusted source for the XSS/injection findings.
This is the access-control mitigation itself; it does not stop authenticated-user-crafted links from targeting a victim, which is noted in the XSS finding.
Verbatim client value into a response header -> response-splitting/header-injection vector.
Client-chosen Content-Type -> reflected XSS via MIME override; strongest finding.
Stored attribute values mapped into headers; NUL-trim loop is not CRLF sanitization.
Bounds-safe (len && guard, len only decreases); risk is false-sanitization, folded into the mapped-attribute finding rather than an OOB finding.
Uploader-controlled stored content type drives browser rendering -> stored XSS.
Emits a fixed literal header name/value; no attacker data reflected.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: user-metadata reflection name += sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1; dump_header(s, name, iter->second) (145-149)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Attacker-derived header NAME and VALUE reflected -> stored header-injection/XSS; also a prefix-length pointer-arithmetic assumption.
Decodes wrapped in try/catch on buffer::error (robust against hostile blobs); emitted values are numeric counts or server-defined enum strings, low injection surface.
The actual sink where accumulated attacker-controlled response_attrs reach the wire; anchors the header-injection finding.
Default binary/octet-stream is safe; the attacker-controlled path into end_header is already captured by the content-type findings.
Body content only present for internal system requests; JSON-encoded server-side.
Unchecked signed offset/length slice into the bufferlist -> potential out-of-bounds read if upstream bounds are wrong.

findings.title: Reflected XSS via client-controlled response-content-type (MIME override)
findings.bug_class: Cross-site scripting via attacker-controlled response Content-Type
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp (Content-Type chosen here, emitted by end_header at line 192; sink end_header in implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: The MIME type used to render an object to a browser must not be freely chosen by a request/uploader in a way that lets attacker-influenced object bytes execute as script in the endpoint origin; responses must be hardened with X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff and a safe Content-Disposition, and script-executing content types must be constrained.
findings.failure_mode: A remote client supplies response-content-type=text/html (val at line 105). It is stored in content_type_str and passed to end_header at line 192 as the response Content-Type. When a victim's browser loads the crafted GET URL, attacker-influenced object content (public/shared bucket object, uploaded HTML/SVG) is rendered as HTML and any embedded script executes in the RGW endpoint origin, enabling session/credential theft. The anonymous check at 109-111 does not prevent an authenticated user from crafting the link for a victim.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether end_header/the frontend forces X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff and/or restricts overridable content types, and whether object content and the RGW UI share an origin that makes rendering exploitable.

findings.title: HTTP response-header injection / response splitting via reflected response-* values
findings.bug_class: HTTP response header injection / response splitting (CRLF)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp (value captured at 113/134, emitted at 181-183; sanitization decision lives in dump_header, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: No value written as an HTTP header may contain CR, LF, or NUL; the header-emit path (dump_header) must reject or strip control characters before serialization.
findings.failure_mode: Client query parameters such as response-content-disposition (val at line 105) are stored verbatim into response_attrs at line 113 and emitted at lines 181-183 via dump_header. If dump_header does not strip CR/LF, embedding \r\n in the value injects arbitrary additional response headers or a fabricated body (response splitting), enabling XSS, cookie setting, and cache poisoning. This is latent: it depends on the unshown dump_header sink.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether dump_header (and end_header) validate/strip CR/LF/NUL in header values; if not, every reflected response-* parameter is an injection vector.

findings.title: Stored header-injection / stored XSS via reflected user object metadata (name and value)
findings.bug_class: Stored XSS / header injection via reflected attacker-controlled header name and value
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp (reflected here; sanitization in dump_header, implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: Both the header NAME derived from a stored attribute key and its VALUE must be validated (no control characters, safe token charset) before being emitted, since an uploader is a different, untrusted principal from the reader for shared/public/cross-tenant buckets.
findings.failure_mode: An uploader stores x-amz-meta-* metadata whose key or value contains CR/LF or an HTML/script payload. On any GET, name (key with prefix stripped at line 148) and iter->second are emitted verbatim at line 149, delivering stored header injection / stored XSS to every reader. Secondary concern: the pointer bump name += sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 assumes RGW_ATTR_PREFIX is a strict shorter prefix of RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX (which was strncmp-matched); if that macro relationship changes, the emitted name could be miscomputed.
findings.what_to_confirm: That dump_header sanitizes both header name and value, and that sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 <= sizeof(RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX)-1 with RGW_ATTR_PREFIX an actual prefix of RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX.

findings.title: Stored Content-Type XSS from object content-type attribute
findings.bug_class: Cross-site scripting via uploader-controlled stored Content-Type
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp (content type chosen here, emitted at end_header line 192; sink not provided)
findings.invariant: A stored object's declared content type must not be able to coerce a reader's browser into executing the object body as script; nosniff and disposition hardening must apply regardless of the stored value.
findings.failure_mode: An uploader sets the object content type to text/html (or another executable type). When no response-content-type override is present, that stored value becomes the response Content-Type at line 192, and a reader who views the object in a browser executes attacker-supplied markup/script in the endpoint origin.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the frontend forces nosniff / safe disposition so stored content types cannot drive script execution for readers.

findings.title: Unchecked signed offset/length slice into object bufferlist
findings.anchor: int r = dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len);
findings.code:
if (get_data && !op_ret) {
    int r = dump_body(s, bl.c_str() + bl_ofs, bl_len);
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read from unvalidated buffer slice
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0210_cwe79.cpp (bounds guaranteed, if at all, by the caller/range pipeline; implementation not provided)
findings.invariant: bl_ofs >= 0, bl_len >= 0, and bl_ofs + bl_len <= bl.length() must hold before reading bl.c_str() + bl_ofs for bl_len bytes.
findings.failure_mode: off_t is signed; if bl_ofs or bl_len is negative or oversized (e.g. from a range-computation error), dump_body reads outside the bufferlist, disclosing adjacent memory or crashing. In normal operation these are computed server-side from validated range state, so exploitability depends on upstream correctness, hence low severity.
findings.what_to_confirm: That the read/range pipeline guarantees bl_ofs and bl_len are non-negative and within bl.length() before this call.

audit_checklist: Confirm dump_header and end_header strip/reject CR, LF, and NUL in both header names and values; without this, every reflected response-* parameter and every x-amz-meta-* attribute is a response-splitting/injection vector (lines 113, 149, 181-183).

Confirm the S3 frontend forces X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff (and a safe Content-Disposition) so client- or uploader-chosen Content-Type (lines 116, 140, 192) cannot make browsers render object content as HTML/script.
Verify whether object content and any trusted RGW web UI share an origin; if so, MIME-override XSS is directly exploitable against authenticated sessions.
Check that the anonymous-only rejection at 109-111 is the intended access model and that authenticated-user-crafted links to a victim are considered in the threat model.
Verify RGW_ATTR_PREFIX is a strict shorter prefix of RGW_ATTR_META_PREFIX so name += sizeof(RGW_ATTR_PREFIX)-1 (line 148) never advances past the matched region.
Confirm bl_ofs/bl_len are validated non-negative and within bl.length() before dump_body at line 202.
Confirm the trailing-NUL trim loop (130-133) is not relied upon as sanitization; interior CR/LF survive it and reach the header sink.
